items.q: Do all Christians read the Bible literally?
items.correct: No — some Christians read it literally as the exact word of God, while liberal Christians treat parts as symbolic or shaped by human authors; interpretation varies widely.
items.misconception: Every Christian believes every word of the Bible is literally true.
items.random_wrong: Christians are forbidden from reading the Bible without a priest present.
items.why: Students generalise the fundamentalist position to all Christians, missing the spectrum from literalist to liberal interpretation that exam answers must acknowledge.

items.q: Do Muslims believe Muhammad wrote the Qur'an?
items.correct: No — Muslims believe the Qur'an is the literal word of Allah revealed to Muhammad through the angel Jibril; Muhammad is the messenger, not the author.
items.misconception: Muhammad composed the Qur'an himself as a record of his own teachings.
items.random_wrong: The Qur'an was originally written in Latin and later translated into Arabic.
items.why: Students apply a human-author model of scripture to Islam, but Muslim belief in the Qur'an's divine authorship is exactly what gives it supreme authority.

items.q: Is conscience always a reliable source of authority for believers?
items.correct: Many traditions teach conscience must be informed by scripture and church teaching first; Catholics, for example, must educate their conscience before following it.
items.misconception: Religious believers think any gut feeling counts as the voice of God.
items.random_wrong: Conscience is a written document kept by each church.
items.why: Students equate conscience with mere instinct, but religious ethics treats it as a faculty that needs forming through scripture, tradition and reason.

items.q: For Catholics, which authority officially interprets scripture and tradition?
items.correct: The Magisterium — the Pope and bishops
items.why: It's The Magisterium — the Pope and bishops. “Each individual believer alone” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How do Muslims regard the Qur'an?
items.correct: As the direct word of Allah revealed to Muhammad
items.misconception: As a biography of Muhammad written centuries later
items.random_wrong: As a translation of the Torah
items.why: The answer examiners want is: As the direct word of Allah revealed to Muhammad. “As a biography of Muhammad written centuries later” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The first five books of the Hebrew Bible
items.why: Correct answer: The first five books of the Hebrew Bible. “A commentary on the Talmud” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In religious ethics, conscience is often described as what?
items.correct: An inner, God-given sense of right and wrong
items.misconception: A legal ruling issued by a court
items.random_wrong: A tax paid to the church
items.why: It's An inner, God-given sense of right and wrong. “A legal ruling issued by a court” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many books are in the Protestant Bible?
items.why: The real figure is 66 books. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
